skill.name: security-compliance
skill.description: Guides security and compliance teams through security architecture, control mapping, threat modeling, risk assessment, incident response, and secure SDLC practices.
skill.summary: Plan security programs, map compliance controls, assess risk, and prepare incident response work.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high-risk patterns, mostly from defensive security documentation, incident response examples, and compliance scripts. Manual review found no prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malware delivery, or confirmed malicious intent. The skill remains medium risk because it contains dual-use offensive security terminology, tool references, and runnable local CSV utilities.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use offensive security references
medium_findings.description: The threat modeling reference lists penetration testing phases, exploitation tools, persistence concepts, C2, and credential harvesting. The material is framed as authorized assessment guidance, but it can inform misuse if copied outside a controlled context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain clear dual-use tool and technique lists. Context shows security assessment education, so the risk is misuse potential rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Educational vulnerable command execution example
medium_findings.description: The application security guide includes a deliberately vulnerable os.system example and a safer subprocess example. This is useful training content, but copied vulnerable code would create command injection risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The vulnerable pattern is explicit and located in a code example. Nearby context labels it as vulnerable and provides a safer alternative, reducing malicious-intent confidence.

medium_findings.title: Incident response content includes ransomware scenarios
medium_findings.description: The skill discusses ransomware incidents, ransom notes, and recovery decision points in templates and tabletop exercises. The context is defensive response planning, but the topic triggered critical static keywords.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static ransomware keywords are real, and sampled context confirms incident response use. I did not find instructions to create or deploy ransomware.

low_findings.title: Local report scripts write user-selected files
low_findings.description: The bundled Python utilities read local CSV input and write optional report files. This is expected behavior for risk and vulnerability reporting, with no network transmission found in the scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly visible in the reporting functions. The scripts are narrow CLI tools and do not show exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: API key and token examples are documentation samples
low_findings.description: Several examples reference API keys, GitHub tokens, or Okta tokens for evidence collection and CI workflows. They are documentation examples rather than hardcoded live secrets, but users should avoid pasting real secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are in example documentation and CI configuration snippets. No evidence found that real credentials are embedded or transmitted by the skill.

low_findings.title: Prompt injection indicators not confirmed
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override instructions, fake system messages, and claims of prior approval found no prompt injection attempt. One pre-approved phrase appears in a normal communication-template context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious phrase search produced one benign result. No evidence found of instructions asking the model to ignore or override the evaluator.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented shell and subprocess execution patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include command execution examples for security automation and secure coding lessons. Most are documentation snippets, but users should review commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Static command execution findings are present in real examples. Manual review indicates training and automation context, not hidden execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Defensive remediation commands modify persistence locations
dangerous_patterns.description: The incident response guide includes commands for deleting malicious scheduled tasks, registry run keys, and cron entries. These are defensive cleanup actions but touch sensitive persistence mechanisms.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and target persistence locations. The surrounding text describes malware removal and containment, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded network examples in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include URLs and IP ranges for API calls, security tools, and penetration test scope. They appear illustrative, but users should not reuse them without validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are present, but sampled context shows examples and scope placeholders. No evidence found of unauthorized outbound calls in executable scripts.

content.value_statement: Security teams need practical structure for audits, risk reviews, and incident response. This skill provides frameworks, templates, and scripts for repeatable security compliance work.
actual_capabilities: Explains security architecture patterns such as Zero Trust, defense in depth, and network segmentation.
Maps common compliance frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and FedRAMP.
Provides incident response templates, tabletop scenarios, and after action report structures.
Includes Python scripts for CSV-based risk scoring and vulnerability prioritization.
Documents secure SDLC practices, OWASP risks, DevSecOps checks, and control evidence workflows.
limitations: It is guidance material, not a substitute for legal, audit, or incident response counsel.
The scripts require user-provided CSV files and do not validate every business context.
Some references are dual-use and require authorized security use only.
Compliance mappings must be reviewed against current regulations and auditor requirements.

use_cases.title: Prepare for SOC 2 readiness
use_cases.description: Use the control examples and roadmap to plan evidence collection, control owners, and audit milestones.

use_cases.title: Prioritize security risks
use_cases.description: Use the risk calculator and methodology notes to rank risks by qualitative and quantitative impact.

use_cases.title: Run incident response exercises
use_cases.description: Use the response templates and tabletop scenarios to test roles, escalation, recovery, and communication plans.

prompt_templates.title: Identify compliance scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me identify likely compliance frameworks for a SaaS product that stores customer data in the United States and Europe.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a risk register
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a risk register outline for our payment application. Include risk names, owners, impact, likelihood, and treatment options.

prompt_templates.title: Review incident response readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess our incident response plan for ransomware readiness. Focus on containment, backup validation, communications, and executive decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Map controls across frameworks
prompt_templates.prompt: Map these access control procedures to SOC 2, ISO 27001, and NIST 800-53. Identify evidence gaps and owners.

output_examples.input: Assess whether our SaaS company is ready for SOC 2 Type II.
output_examples.output: A readiness plan with scope, control owners, evidence needs, timeline, gaps, and audit preparation tasks.
A ranked remediation list with priority levels, due dates, business rationale, and compensating control notes.

output_examples.input: Create a ransomware tabletop exercise for leadership and IT.
output_examples.output: A scenario agenda with timed injects, expected decisions, communication checkpoints, and after action topics.

best_practices: Use the templates with real asset inventories, owners, and regulatory obligations.
Validate generated plans with legal, audit, privacy, and incident response stakeholders.
Run scripts on trusted CSV inputs and review output before sharing reports.
anti_patterns: Do not treat framework mappings as final audit evidence without review.
Do not use dual-use security references outside authorized environments.
Do not paste secrets, production tokens, or confidential incident data into prompts.

faq.question: Can this skill certify SOC 2 or ISO 27001 compliance?
faq.answer: No. It helps prepare evidence and plans, but certification requires qualified auditors and formal assessment.

faq.question: Does it run automated security scans?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and local CSV analysis scripts, not a full scanner or monitoring platform.

faq.question: Can I use it during an active incident?
faq.answer: Yes, for structure and checklists. Engage your incident response, legal, and communications teams for decisions.

faq.question: Are the included scripts safe to run?
faq.answer: They process local CSV data and write reports. Review inputs and outputs before using them in production workflows.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It includes dual-use security terminology and examples. The content is defensive, but users should apply it only with authorization.

faq.question: Which assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
